event: Moses breaks the tablets of the Law
symbols: stone tablets raised to be smashed — the broken covenant between God and Israel due to the golden calf idolatry (Exodus 32:19)
Moses' uplifted arm — righteous anger and prophetic zeal on behalf of God's honor
barren rocky mountaintop — Mount Sinai, the place of divine revelation and the giving of the Law
white and red garments — prophetic authority and the fiery zeal of God's chosen mediator
solitary figure against vast landscape — the loneliness of prophetic leadership, standing between God and a faithless people
description: A dramatic scene of an elderly bearded man in flowing white and red-orange robes standing on a rocky mountain ledge. He raises his right arm high above his head, gripping a stone tablet that he is about to hurl downward. His face is turned upward with an expression of anguished fury. His left hand appears to grasp or push away the figure of another person in white garments who clings to him, possibly trying to restrain him or falling back from the force. The barren, craggy mountainside fills the background under a moody sky. The composition conveys explosive energy and prophetic wrath.
theological_description: This image depicts the climactic moment of Exodus 32:19, when Moses descends from Mount Sinai bearing the two tablets of the covenant inscribed by the finger of God, only to discover Israel worshipping the golden calf. In righteous fury, Moses 'threw the tablets out of his hands, breaking them to pieces at the foot of the mountain.' The breaking of the tablets is one of the most theologically charged acts in the Hebrew Bible: it signifies the rupture of the covenant relationship between God and his people caused by idolatry. The Decalogue, written by God himself (Exodus 31:18), represented the terms of the Sinai covenant -- Israel's identity as a holy nation and a kingdom of priests (Exodus 19:6). Their destruction dramatizes the devastating consequences of unfaithfulness. The rabbinical tradition (Talmud, Shabbat 87a) understood Moses' act as divinely sanctioned: just as a marriage contract becomes void when one party is unfaithful, so the covenant tablets were shattered because Israel had already broken faith. Yet the narrative does not end in destruction: God commands Moses to cut two new tablets (Exodus 34:1), signaling divine mercy and the renewal of the covenant. This trajectory from broken covenant to restoration prefigures the New Covenant prophesied in Jeremiah 31:31-33, in which God writes his law not on stone but on the human heart.

scripture_refs.reason: The painting directly depicts Moses shattering the tablets of the Law upon seeing Israel's idolatry.
The tablets Moses raises were written by the finger of God, underscoring the gravity of their destruction.
The promise of replacement tablets -- God's mercy beyond judgment -- provides the narrative's theological resolution.
The broken stone tablets prefigure the New Covenant in which God writes his law on hearts rather than stone.
Paul contrasts the ministry of death carved in letters on stone with the ministry of the Spirit written on human hearts.
The broken tablets signify the jeopardized status of Israel as God's treasured possession and priestly kingdom.
